coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of graphene quantum dots/CoNiAl-layered double-hydroxide nanocomposite: Application as a glucose sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Samuei, Sara
Fakkar, Jila
Rezvani, Zolfaghar
Shomali, Ashkan
Habibi, Biuck
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, a novel nanocomposite based on the graphene quantum dots and CoNiAl-layered double-hydroxide was successfully synthesized by co-precipitation method. To achieve the morphological, structural and compositional information, the resulted nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy X-ray diffraction, thermal gravimetric analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and photoluminescence. Then, the nanocomposite was used as a modifier to fabricate a modified carbon paste electrode as a non-enzymatic sensor for glucose determination. Electrochemical behavior and determination of glucose at the nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode were investigated by cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry methods, respectively. The prepared sensor offered good electrocatalytic properties, fast response time, high reproducibility and stability. At the optimum conditions, the constructed sensor exhibits wide linear range; 0.01–14.0 mM with a detection limit of 6 μM (S/N = 3) and high sensitivity of 48.717 μAmM−1. Finally, the sensor was successfully applied to determine the glucose in real samples which demonstrated its applicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Layered double-hydroxide
Electrocatalytic
Sensor
Non-enzymatic
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the modified carbon paste electrode
Electrocatalytic activity of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE towards glucose
Amperometric detection of glucose by GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE
Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of (a) GQDs, (b) alone CoNiAl-LDH and (c) GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite. (B) FT-IR spectrums of (a) GQDs, (b) alone CoNiAl-LDH, and (c) GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite. (C) PL spectrums of pure GQDs (a) and GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite. (D) SEM images of (a) alone CoNiAl-LDH and (b) GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite in two different magnifications (L = low and R = high magnification).
TGA (curves a) and DTA (curves b) of (A) alone CoNiAl-LDH and (B) GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (a), CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (b) and bare CPE (c) in 0.1 M NaOH solution. (B) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE in the absence (a) and presence (b) of the 1.0 mM glucose in 0.1 M NaOH solution. Inset in B: Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (a) and CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (b) in 0.1 M NaOH containing 1.0 mM glucose. Scan rate, 50 mV s−1.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE in 0.1 M NaOH at different concentrations of glucose in the ranges of 1.0 mM–10.0 mM (I) and plot of anodic peak currents vs concentrations of glucose (II). (B) Cyclic voltammograms of 1.0 mM glucose at the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE in different scan rates at the ranges of 5–750 mV/s (I) and relationship between the catalytic currents vs square root of the scan rate (II).

Typical current–time responses of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE to successive addition of glucose in a stirred condition (0.1 M NaOH, at 25 °C and applied potential = 0.1 V). Inset: Plot of amperometric current vs. glucose concentrations.

(A) Stability study of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE for 0.02 mM glucose under continues stirring for 1000 s (0.1 M NaOH, at 25 °C and applied potential = 0.1 V) and (B) Amperometric responses of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE to the successive addition of 0.02 mM glucose, 0.05 mM AA and UA as interferes and finally again 0.02 mM glucose.

Amperometric responses of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE towards two types of fruit juice samples (A: orange juice and B: mango juice) step 1 and successive additions of 0.02 mM of glucose standard solution in 0.1 M NaOH at an applied potential of 0.1 V. Insets show the linear dependence of currents as a function of glucose concentrations.
Analytical performance of the different glucose sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CHIT/GOx/NiO/GCE: chitosan-glucose oxidase-NiO hollow nanosphere-modified GC electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of glucose in fruit juice samples by the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE via amperometric method.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of graphene quantum dots/CoNiAl-layered double-hydroxide nanocomposite: Application as a glucose sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, 53714-161, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, a novel nanocomposite based on the graphene quantum dots and CoNiAl-layered double-hydroxide was successfully synthesized by co-precipitation method. To achieve the morphological, structural and compositional information, the resulted nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy X-ray diffraction, thermal gravimetric analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and photoluminescence. Then, the nanocomposite was used as a modifier to fabricate a modified carbon paste electrode as a non-enzymatic sensor for glucose determination. Electrochemical behavior and determination of glucose at the nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode were investigated by cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry methods, respectively. The prepared sensor offered good electrocatalytic properties, fast response time, high reproducibility and stability. At the optimum conditions, the constructed sensor exhibits wide linear range; 0.01–14.0 mM with a detection limit of 6 μM (S/N = 3) and high sensitivity of 48.717 μAmM−1. Finally, the sensor was successfully applied to determine the glucose in real samples which demonstrated its applicability.

Proposed structure of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The glucose detection has an important role in the human's lives. Despite of great development in the medicine sciences, diabetes has become one of the significant health problems of the peoples in the world and the numbers of mans suffering from the diabetic patients are increasing day to day [1]. Thus, by increasing request for monitoring devices by humans, it is important to develop the fast, accurate and stable analytical methods to detect the glucose levels [2]. For decades, electroanalyzing of the glucose level in blood, foods and medicines has been an attractive method [3]. The most of these types of analysis involve application of the enzymatic modified electrodes based on the application of glucose oxidase enzyme as a glucose sensing material. Although these modified electrodes work at a wide range of pH and act high selective, they have the limited applications due to the variable sensitivity and low stability. Therefore, non-enzymatic glucose sensors and bio-sensors have been introduced to the relevant scientific community [4]. Non-enzymatic glucose sensors and bio-sensors have been reported to represent significantly higher sensitivity and stability than enzymatic ones and they have been developed at a considerable rate in the past decades. To review and presentation of such modified electrodes, Zang et al. [3], Park et al. [5], Si al [6] and Toghill et al. [7] have been published the excellent and authoritative review articles regarding the development of non-enzymatic glucose sensors and bio-sensors.

Recently, layered double-hydroxides (LDHs) known as anionic or hydrotalcite-like clays have attracted more attention due to their desirable biocompatibility [8], intense absorbability [9], high catalytic activity [10], chemical stability [11,12] and low cost [13]. LDHs have been demonstrated by the previous studies to be suitable materials for the designing of electrochemical bio-sensors through immobilization of various proteins or enzymes [14,15]. Additionally, they are highly efficient in transferring electrons and enhancing the electrode-electrolyte contact area due to their high surface area-to-volume ratio [16]. The general formula: [M
2+
1−x
M
3+
x
(OH)
2
]
x+
(A
n−
)
x/n
·mH
2
O, describes LDHs. here M
2+ and M
3+ are divalent and trivalent cations, respectively, A
n− is an anion to be exchanged, and x is a ratio of M
3+
/(M
2+
+M
3+
) and the M
2+
/M
3+ ratio determines charge of the layers [17,18]. On the other hand, the layers charge will depend on the M
2+
/M
3+ ratio [17,18]. As it has been reported, the NiAl-LDH [19] and CoAl-LDH [20] as binary components represent high-performance materials for LDHs based modified electrodes. However, due to the enhancing of the electrochemical active sites caused by Co and Ni cations as two types of transition metals [21], the NiCoAl-LDH [22] as ternary component has been found to be more efficient compared to the mentioned binary-component LDHs. Despite of these advantages, fabrication of modified electrodes using LDHs is seriously limited due to the low conductivity of the these materials which affect ionic electron transferring [20,23]. In practice, to solve the problem of the low conductivity, addition of the carbon materials that are highly conductive and in some cases, such as carbon nanotubes and carbon quantum dots, in addition to high conductivity, have hybrid effects to enhance the electrochemical performances [24], is an efficient and effective strategy in the preparation of LDHs based modified electrodes and improving their applications in electrochemical sensing. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new class of zero dimensional carbon materials, have been intensively investigated as an ideal substrate to grow and anchor functional nanomaterials. Over the past two decades [25], a great interest has been attracted by GQDs because of their intrinsic properties, such as biocompatibility, abundant availability, high stability without photobleaching, low toxicity, and providing green and facile synthesis methods [26–32]. Moreover, compared to the other inorganic quantum dots acting as semiconductors, they are highly soluble in different solvents due to the existence of functional groups at their edges. So that, resulted nanohybrid and nanocomposite materials exhibit enhanced electrochemical performance, demonstrating that GQDs are able to serve as the zero-dimensional components to facilitate the electron transport rate, high electrolyte contact area, conductivity, and structural stability. On the other hand, the enhancements could be attributed to the cooperative contributions from GQDs and the other nanostructures such as LDHs, which is highly desired in emerging fields such as high-performance electrocatalytic and electrochemical devices [33,34]. So far, preparation of the GQDs has been done through several methods and can be categorized into the two main top-down and bottom-up [35–37] groups. In the first groups, a large amount of precursors such as graphene sheets of micrometer sizes, graphite nanoparticles, graphene oxide nanoparticles, nanodiamonds, carbon nanotubes, and carbon fibers are decomposed and GQDs are formed [38–40]. Meanwhile, in the second groups a modified bottom-up approach was employed by Huang et al., who adopted a new strategy for the easy synthesis of GQDs with high qualities and few surface defects. In this procedure, high and low oxygenated GQDs were separated [34] by a solvent following the microwave pyrolysis of carbohydrate molecules.

In the current work, a novel nanocomposite of GQDs; the GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysing of the citric acid, and CoNiAl-LDH was successfully prepared in alkaline medium through the co-precipitation method. To obtain the morphological, structural, compositional and electrochemical information, the prepared nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, thermal gravimetric analysis, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, photoluminescence, and electrochemical techniques. Then, the nanocomposite was used as the modifier to fabricate a modified carbon paste electrode, GQDs/CoNiAl-LDHs/CPE, for non-enzymatic determination of glucose. The obtained nanocomposite modified electrode exhibited a significant synergistic electrocatalytic activity towards the glucose oxidation compared to CoNiAl-LDH alone modified electrode. The amperometric response increased linearly with increasing glucose concentration on the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDHs/CPE which opens up new opportunities for fast, simple and sensitive determination of glucose. The GQDs/CoNiAl-LDHs/CPE, glucose sensor, was successfully applied for the analysis of glucose in real samples; demonstrating the applicability of the prepared sensor.

All of the used chemicals such as Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O and Al(NO3)3·9H2O, NaOH and HCl were of analytical grade and obtained from Merck company and all steps of synthesis and analysis were conducted using double distilled water. The pH values were adjusted by combining different amounts of 0.1 M solutions of NaOH and 0.1 M solution of HCl. Citric acid and glucose was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company and used as received.

AUTOLAB PGSTAT-100 (potentiostat/galvanostat) (www.metrohm-autolab.com) with three-electrode system; GQDs/CoNiAl-LDHs/CPE, a silver/silver chloride electrode [Ag/AgCl/KCl (3.0 M)] (Azar Electrode Co., Iran) and a platinum wire electrode (Azar Electrode Co., Iran) respectively as working, reference and counter electrodes, was used to carry out the electrochemical studies. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded by the Bruker AXS (Germany) model D8 advanced diffractometer for Cu Kα radiation (l = 1.54 A˚) at 40 kV and 35 mA with Bragg angle ranging from 3 to 70°. The FT-IR spectra were obtained by using a Bruker (Germany) spectrophotometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 and KBr disk method at room temperature. The pH values were measured with Hana pH-meter model 211. The thermogravimetric (TGA) curves were recorded by a Mettler Toledo TGA 851e device with heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere. The morphology of the samples was observed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM; KYKY-EM3200) at 30 keV. Photoluminescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded using a JASCO luminescence spectrometer.

The GQDs was directly synthesized by pyrolyzing of the citric acid. In a typical route, 2.0 g citric acid was put into autoclave and heated to 180 °C. About 30 min later, the citric acid molecules were pyrolyzed, cross-linked, and finally converted into carbon nanoparticles as a pale yellow liquid. Pyrolysis process continues as temperatures increase by more heating and the color of solution in reaction flask changes from yellow to orange as a result of formation of GQDs. Then 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution was added dropwise into the obtained orange liquid solution under vigorous stirring [41]. After neutralization of pH to 7.0, solvent treatment separation process was performed by equal volume of chloroform and double distilled water. By this way, the aqueous phase of the reaction flask that contain GQDs with the high oxygen-related groups on the surface was separated and used for preparing of the nanocomposite [34]. The aqueous orange solution of GQDs was also kept at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

The GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite was prepared by the co-precipitation method. Typically, 30 ml of an aqueous solution containing Ni (NO3)2·6H2O, Co (NO3)2·6H2O and Al (NO3)3·9H2O, (Co:Ni:Al = 1:2:1) was prepared and added dropwise into a 20 ml solution containing the sonicated GQDs, under vigorous stirring conditions. During the addition of the metal ions solutions, the pH of the reaction mixture was maintained at 10.0 using 2 M NaOH solution. The reaction carried out under N2 atmosphere while the solution temperature was maintained at 70 °C. The obtained product was aged for 24 h. Then the resulting slurry was filtered, washed three times with double distilled water and dried at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the modified carbon paste electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of modified carbon paste electrode was carried out as follows: the construction materials including: GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite, graphite and paraffin were hand mixed in a mass ratio of 64:32:8 with a mortar and pestle until a uniformly wet paste was obtained. Then a portion of the homogeneous resulting paste was packed firmly into the cavity (1.6 mm diameter) of a glass tube. The electrical contact was established via a copper wire fitted into the glass tube. The surface of electrode was smoothed on a glossy paper and rinsed with double distilled water prior to each experiment.

Fig. 1 A shows XRD patterns of the synthesized GQDs, alone CoNiAl-LDH and prepared GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite. The XRD pattern of synthesized GQDs (Fig. 1A curve a) exhibits a broad peak near to 2θ = 27°, belonging to the (002) plane of graphene with a relatively low intensity. Fig. 1A curve b displays the XRD pattern of alone CoNiAl-LDH. Symmetric reflections for (003), (006), and (110) are exposed by the CoNiAl-LDH sample, while representing a hexagonal lattice of R3m symmetry [42]. Broad asymmetric peaks for (009), (015), (018) and (113) planes, these are the characteristics of the hydrotalcite-like compounds (JSPDC no. 15-0087) [43]. Calculation of the lattice distances and determination of their parameters “c” and “a” [13] are made possible by the characteristic reflections of CoNiAl-LDH structure of (003), (006), and (110) planes. Parameter “c” is corresponding to 3 times of basal spacing between the adjacent brucite-like layers. It can be calculated by equations c = d (003) + 2d (006) + 3d (009) [13]. The separation of the two neighboring LDH layers can be nearly represented by d (003) value including thicknesses of LDH layers and their interlayer regions. The mean of cation-cation distance within brucite-like layers serves as a function of the value of parameter “a”, calculated as a = 2d (110) [44]. The value of lattice parameters “a” is a function of the mean cation–cation distance within brucite-like layers, which can be calculated as a = 2d (110) [44]. In the case of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite, the diffraction peaks are similar to those of the alone CoNiAl-LDH, but no shifts in the basal reflection peak were observed (Fig. 1A curve c). We can conclude that there has been a similarity between the alone CoNiAl-LDH and nanocomposite within the interlayer anion galleries. Comparison of the XRD patterns clearly displays a decrease in the intensities of (003) and (009) reflection peaks for the nanocomposite, which may be related to the disorder in the stacked structure [45]. In addition, no extra characteristic peaks were detected from the all XRD patterns, indicating that there are no impurities in the products. The proposed structure of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite which was drowned by Materials studio software package is shown in Scheme 1 . In this scheme GQDs and nitrate anions are located on the surface and between of the layers, respectively.

The FT-IR spectrums of synthesized GQDs (curve a), alone CoNiAl-LDH (curve b) and GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite (curve c) at room temperature are shown in Fig. 1B. As can be seen from curve a, there are distinct peaks located approximately at 1723 cm−1 which is assigned to CO stretch of carboxylic functional group in GQDs and the broad peak centered at 3444 cm−1 is related to OH band. It is worth noting that the CC band at 1647 cm−1 was not observed in the synthesized and obtained GQDs [29]. For the alone CoNiAl-LDH, the bands in the low-frequency region; below 1000 cm−1, were appeared due to the lattice vibration modes of metal–oxygen–metal stretching [44] such as the M–O–H vibration centered at 575.81 cm−1 and the O–M–O vibration bond around 426.50 cm−1 [46,47] (spectrum b). On the other hand, the band with peak maxima at 1384 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of NO3 − anions in the interlayer gallery and the strong broad band centered at 3427.91 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups of both surface and interlayer water molecules in CoNiAl-LDH [48]. Due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between interlayer water and hydroxyl groups with anions of OH bands, the CoNiAl-LDHs were observed at lower frequencies compared with those of free water [49]. Compared to the pristine CoNiAl-LDH and pure GQDs, the spectrum pattern of the nanocomposite have additional strong interactions at spectrum c, which were related to simultaneous characteristic signals of pure CoNiAl-LDHs and GQDs, such as carbonyl and nitrate peaks confirming that GQDs had not entered in the interlayer galleries but existed on CoNiAl-LDH surfaces.

Photoluminescence (PL) studies were carried out for the future investigation of the obtained GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite. The photoactive guest molecules assembled onto the CoNiAl-LDH layers could achieve new types of composite materials with advanced photo-related performances, such as luminescence [50]. As reported by Xia and et al., occurrence of emission shift, line broadening and even luminescence quenching [51] in the prepared nanocomposite are the result of the aggregates of luminescent molecules in a solid state matrix. Fig. 1C shows the PL spectra of synthesized GQDs (curve a) and GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite (curve b) with maximum exited wavelengths (λ max) located at 380 nm. As can be seen, the considerable decay (or quenching) of PL intensity in the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite compared with that of synthesized GQDs accrued which can be result via electron transfers from GQDs to CoNiAl-LDH [52]. Furthermore, the significant blue shift at the emission wavelength from 473 nm to 360 nm can be attributed to strong intermolecular interactions between synthesized GQDs and CoNiAl-LDH layers [53] in the nanocomposite structure construction.

SEM was employed to explore the surface morphology of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite and also the alone CoNiAl-LDH. As can be seen (Fig. 1D), the CoNiAl-LDH morphology (a, at two different magnifications: L low and R high magnification) consists of irregular particles with rough surfaces that are concluded to be agglomerated. It is worth noting that the nanocomposite surface morphology (Fig. 1D, b, at two various magnifications: L low and R high magnifications) is significantly different from those of alone CoNiAl-LDH. In the SEM image of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite, it can be seen that GQDs completely covers the surfaces of precursor CoNiAl-LDH with a partly uniform spherical morphology.

The results of the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA) for the alone CoNiAl-LDH and GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 2 (A) and (B), respectively. For the alone CoNiAl-LDH (Fig. 2A) two stages of decomposition can be observed (curve a). The first step incorporates the release water from the surface and LDH interlayer within the ranges of 50–180 °C. The second step corresponds to the dehydroxylation of brucite-like octahedral layers within the ranges of 180–350 °C. In the DTA, two distinct peaks were appeared for the alone CoNiAl-LDH. The first peak is attributed to the elimination of water molecules at 110 °C and the second peak is resulted from the dehydration of CoNiAl-LDH layers around 250 °C. For the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite (Fig. 2 B) there is a significant shift towards high temperatures for the decomposition steps (curve a and b correspond to TGA and DTA results), e.g. the first and the second stages were accrued at 202 °C and 286 °C correspond to water releasing and dehydroxilation, respectively. Comparison of the weight loss pattern of the alone CoNiAl-LDH and GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite indicates that the latter has a more thermal stability.

Fig. 3 A displays the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (curve a) and alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (without GQDs) (curve b) and bare CPE (curve c) in 0.1 M NaOH solution. As can be seen (curve c), bare CPE did not represent any electrochemical activities within the considered potential ranges, while the Ni (III)/Ni (II) couple in the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE and also alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE are electroactive in the alkaline media and produces the waves of I and I′ in the CVs (curve a and b). Comparison of the electrochemical behaviors of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE and alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE demonstrated that the oxidation and reduction peak currents of the Ni (III)/Ni (II) couple increase with the asymmetric shape and also the potential between anodic and cathodic parts decreases for the nanocomposite modified electrode. Furthermore, the reduction peak of the Ni (III)/Ni (II) couple in nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode shifts to the positive potentials and shows high reversibility than the same couple in the alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE. The area (Q) under the anodic peak (wave I) of the CVs for GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE and alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE could be used to evaluate the surface coverage of modified carbon paste electrodes (Г) through the following equation [54]:
(1)
Γ = Q / nFA
where n is the number of electrons, F is Faraday constant, and A is the electrode surface area. The value of 4.84 × 10−6 mol cm2 and 2.021 × 10−6 mol cm2 were found for the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE and alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE, respectively. This result indicates that in the presence of GQDs, Ni centers as electroactive species are further spread in the nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode. Moreover, due to the presence of GQDs, electron transfer in the nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode occurs much easier and quicker than in the alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE towards glucose
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrocatalytic performance of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE for glucose oxidation is firstly evaluated by means of cyclic voltammetry in 0.1 M of NaOH solution. Fig. 3B shows the typical CVs of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE in the absence (curve a) and presence (curve b) of 0.1 mM glucose in NaOH solution. In the presence of glucose, an increase in Ni (II) oxidation peak current is observed in the forward scan. At the same time, a decrease in Ni (III) reduction peak current is attended in the backward scan. This reveals that the nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode has good electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation. As reported in the literature, Ni centers can be participate as a redox process and exhibit the best signal of Ni (III)/Ni (II) couple in NaOH solution as shown by Eq. (2) [56]:
(2)
LDH − Ni
( II )
+
OH
solution
−
→ LDH
(
OH
−
)
− Ni
( III )
+ e
−

The following reaction shows the electrooxidation of glucose at the above conditions:
(3)
LDH
(
OH
−
)
− Ni
( III )
+ Glucose → LDH − Ni
( II )
+ Glucolactone
Therefore, reaction of Ni (III) centers as oxidant can be proposed in the electrocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode.

On the other hand, in the presence of glucose a new oxidation peak is occurred at the potential of about 0.0 V vs Ag/AgCl (Fig. 3B curve b). The catalytic current of glucose electrooxidation on the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE has been estimated from the difference between the current measured at the potential of 0.0 V vs Ag/AgCl in the presence and absence of 0.1 mM glucose and has been used for determination of glucose concentrations. Inset of Fig. 3B shows the CVs of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (curve a) and alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (curve b) in the presence of 0.1 mM glucose in NaOH solution. As evidence, the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (curve a) exhibits a much higher anodic peak current for glucose oxidation than the alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (curve b). The anodic peak current at the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE is about 3 times higher than the alone CoNiAl-LDH/CPE (without GQDs). This result suggests that the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite, a novel electrocatalyst material, has high electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation in NaOH solution, and we assume that the high electrocatalytic performance is mainly attributed to the formation of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite. Strong association of the CoNiAl-LDH with CQDs further facilitated charge transport and improved electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation.

The study of the effect of glucose concentration on the new anodic peak current at about 0.0 V vs Ag/AgCl was carried out by cyclic voltammetry in the concentration ranges of 1.0–10.0 mM glucose in NaOH solution with scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (Fig. 4 A). As can be seen from Fig. 4A (I), the oxidation peak current of the glucose at the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE increased gradually with the increasing of glucose concentrations. It has been observed that the increase of anodic peak current is linear with the increase of glucose concentrations [Fig. 4A (II)]. More investigations were done to identify glucose features of transport at the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE surface. For this purpose, the CVs were recorded in the presence 1.0 mM glucose at various scan rates. Fig. 4B (I) exhibits the CVs of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE at different scan rates from 5 to 750 mV s−1 within the potential range of −1.5–1.0 V. The results show that the currents associated with glucose oxidation increase with scan rate. On the other hand, anodic peak potential of glucose oxidation increases very slightly to higher potentials when the scan rate is enhanced from 5 to 750 mV s−1. According to literature [57], this indicates the restriction is caused by the kinetics of charge transfer at higher scan rates. Different factors such as chemical interactions between electrolyte ions, as well as between the redox couples present on the electrode surface, the non-equivalent sites existing in the modified electrode [55], and electrostatic factors play a role in this limitation. As exhibited in Fig. 4B (II), the anodic peak current for glucose oxidation is proportional to the square root of the scan rate. The linear regression equations was expressed as Ip/μA = 30.198υ1/2/(mV s−1)-118.95 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.995. This indicates a diffusion-controlled process for GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric detection of glucose by GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic oxidation of glucose at the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was also studied by chronoamperometry. Fig. 5 shows the amperometric response of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE to successive additions of glucose to 0.1 M NaOH solution under stirring. The current response was linear with glucose concentration within the ranges of 0.01–14.0 mM (inset of Fig. 5). A detection limit of 6 μM glucose was determined at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 and the sensitivity was 48.717 μA mM−1. The GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE responds rapidly to the substrate, glucose, and could achieve 95% of the steady-state current within 6 s. This indicates a fast amperometric response of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE to glucose.

The performances of glucose detection by the present fabricated sensor were compared with other sensors in the literature and the results are shown in Table 1 . It can be seen that GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE offers a reasonable linear range for glucose detection, which is lower than those of some of the previous reports. On the other hand, by considering clinically normal range of blood sugar (4–6 mM) in human blood serum, the analytical applicability of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE sensor developed in this work is promising.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE
ce:section.ce:para: Reproducibility, repeatability and stability of a constructed sensor are essential for practical applications. The reproducibility of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was investigated by comparing the amperometric current response to glucose at five modified electrodes which were prepared independently. The relative standard deviation (RSD) obtained was about 3.08% at a glucose concentration of 0.02 mM, thus indicating an acceptable reproducibility for the present nanocomposite modified electrode. The repeatability of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was also determined in a 0.02 mM glucose solution via five evaluations and RSD value of 2.3% was obtained for the glucose determination. On the other hand, the stability of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was investigated towards electrooxidation of glucose during 1000 s as seen in Fig. 6 A. Due to decreasing glucose concentration on the surface of the modified electrode, anodic current has undergone a poor decline. Also, the stability of the selected GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was examined as follows: The sensor was rinsed with double-distilled water and kept in PBS at pH = 7.0 after recording the oxidation peak current of 0.02 mM glucose at optimum conditions. The response value for 0.02 mM glucose was found to reduce to about 2.0% in a week and almost 6.0% in a month. Thus, the high stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the planned sensor would be proven.

Based on practical applications of amperometric sensors and bio-sensors, the selectivity of the present sensor, GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE, was necessary and should be study. Therefore, an investigation was done on the probable effects of some interfering substances like as uric acid, ascorbic acid, and other carbohydrate compounds, which usually co-exist with glucose in human blood or other real samples and can be easily oxidized at the positive potential of 0.1 V in 0.1 M of NaOH solution. Fig. 6B shows the amperometric i–t response of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE upon the addition of 0.02 mM glucose and 0.05 mM ascorbic acid and uric acid. The GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE response shows no amperometric signal towards ascorbic acid and uric acid. On the other hand, the current responses were hardly discernible after the addition of each mentioned compounds, but a well-defined response was obtained after the addition of glucose in the first and last steps, respectively. The good electrocatalytic behavior of the fabricated sensor associated with a stable and well-defined amperometric response towards glucose in the presence of the interfering species, indicating a much lower response for the ascorbic acid and uric acid compared to that of glucose. These result confirmed that the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE is more sensitive and selective for detection of glucose in the presence of potentially active interfering compounds.

To examine the practical applicability of the developed sensor, GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was used for determination of glucose in fruit Juices. For this propose, two different types of commercially available fruit juice samples including orange and mango juices which were bought from local supermarket in Tabriz, Iran, examined. Both of the commercial fruit juices were centrifugated with 6000 rpm for 30 min and the obtained clear samples were analyzed as follows: 5.0 ml form each one of the clear fruit juices (supernatants) was diluted with 20.0 ml of the 0.1 M NaOH solution as supporting electrolyte with dilution factor about 5 in order to obtain the final real samples. Fig. 7 A and B shows the amperometric responses of successive addition of the final real samples and 0.02 mM glucose as standard solution. By addition of the final real samples and also standard solution of glucose, a fast and remarkable increase was occurred in current, indicating that the sensor has a rapid and sensitive response to changes in glucose concentration. This shows the good electrocatalytic capability of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE and its applicability in the determination of glucose in real samples. It is clear that the determination of glucose in the fruit juices may be affected by the matrix effects because of the complexity of their matrix. On the other hand, the selection and study of individual interference compounds in fruit juice is quite difficult; thus, in order to evaluate the existence of matrix effects, the slopes obtained from the standard additions curves in determination of glucose in fruit juices were compared with the slope obtained in 0.1 M NaOH solution (absence of fruit juices and their matrix) [62]. For both analyzed samples (orange and mango juices), the calculated t values were lower than the critical t value for 4 degrees of freedom (2.78, p = 0.05), thus allowing us to accept the null hypothesis that there are no matrix effect during determination of glucose in fruit juices [63]. The content of glucose in the real samples (undiluted fruit juices) of orange and mango juices was calculated from the calibration curve and the obtained results were listed in Table 2 . As can be seen from Table 2, glucose determination by using GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE would be effective with high sensitivity for the determining of glucose in real samples.

This work presents a simple way to preparation of a novel GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE and its application for glucose sensing in alkaline media. The prepared GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH nanocomposite was characterized by XRD, FT-IR, PL, TGA DTA, and SEM techniques. The electrochemical behaviour of the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE was investigated by cyclic voltammetry. The experimental results indicated that the GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE has an excellent electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation and can be used to fabricate a non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor with a variety of good electrochemical characteristics including high sensitivity, good selectivity, favorable reproducibility, and suitable long-term stability. Such distinctive features of GQDs/CoNiAl-LDH/CPE as a sensor make it capable for the determining of the trace amounts of glucose in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the research office of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University for its financial support.
